title: Today's AI Brief: How Wasmer used Codex to build a Node.js runtime for the edge
summary: AI Brief: Wasmer uses Codex for edge Node.js runtime, Listen Labs raises $69M for AI customer interviews

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI Brief. Let's dive into some exciting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. I've got a couple of stories to share that are making me think about the pace of change in this space.

First up, Wasmer used Codex with GPT-3.5 to build a Node.js runtime for the edge. This move accelerated their development process 10 to 20 times faster, allowing them to ship in weeks instead of months. The use of AI in coding is really starting to show its potential here. I mean, who wouldn't want to speed up their development process that much?

Next, Listen Labs raised $69 million after pulling off a viral billboard hiring stunt to scale AI customer interviews. The company's innovative approach to recruitment and AI-driven customer insights seems to be resonating with investors. It's always fun to see creative approaches to hiring, and $69 million is no small validation of their approach.
And while not directly related, it's worth noting that the AI space is seeing a lot of activity. From coding to customer insights, AI is making a tangible impact across industries.

How Wasmer used Codex to build a Node.js runtime for the edge is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: See how Wasmer used Codex with GPT-5.5 to build a Node.js runtime for the edge, accelerating development 10x to 20x and shipping in weeks instead of months. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Listen Labs raises $69M after viral billboard hiring stunt to scale AI customer interviews is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Alfred Wahlforss was running out of options. His startup, Listen Labs , needed to hire over 100 engineers, but competing against Mark Zuckerberg's $100 million offers seemed impossible. So he spent $5,000 — a fifth of his marketing budget — on a billboard in San Francisco displaying what looked like gibberish: five strings of random numbers. The numbers wer... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
